turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has her opinion in case 16-534, Rubin v. Islamic Republic of Iran.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act grants foreign states in their agencies and instrumentalities immunity from suit in the United States and grants their property immunity from attachment and execution in satisfaction of judgments against them. But those grants of immunity are subject to exception. Petitioners hold a judgment against respondent Islamic Republic of Iran under one such exception that permits suits against foreign states that are designated as state sponsors of terrorism when their claims arise out of acts of terrorism. Iran did not pay the judgment, so petitioners filed an action in the district court to attach and execute against certain Iranian assets including the property at issue here which is a collection of ancient clay tablets and fragments house that responded University of Chicago. The district court concluded that petitioners could not attach and execute against the property because it is not subject to an exception to immunity. The Seventh Circuit agreed and we now affirm. Petitioners contend that Title 28 United States Code Section 1610(g) provides a freestanding exception to property immunity that applies whenever a plaintiff holds a terrorism-related judgment against a foreign state. We do not read Section 1610(g) so broadly. Congress enacted the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act in an effort to codify the careful balance between respecting the immunity historically afforded to foreign sovereigns and holding them accountable for their actions. As a default Section 1609 provides that the property in the United States of a foreign state shall be immune from attachment arrest and execution. Section 1610 outlines exceptions to this immunity. Unlike the immunity stripping provisions elsewhere in Section 1610 however, Section 1610(g) conspicuously lacks language that clearly rescinds immunity of property. To the contrary, Section 1610(g) provides in relevant part that it will apply to certain property "as provided in this section" we understand the words as provided in this section to refer to Section 1610 as a whole so that Section 1610(g) will apply only to property that is expressly exempt from immunity as provided elsewhere in Section 1610. This reading of Section 1610 still provides relief to judgment holders who would not have been able to attach and execute against certain property before the enactment of Section 1610(g). Section 1610(g) unambiguously abrogates in certain circumstances a decision of this Court commonly known as Bancec, which may otherwise have prevented a plaintiff holding a terrorism-related judgment against a foreign state from attaching and executing against the property of an agency or instrumentality of that foreign state. Section 1610(g) serves to identify in one place all the category of property that will be available to plaintiff's holding terrorism-related judgments and commands that the availability of such property will not be limited by Bancec. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion we reject the petitioner's other arguments as unpersuasive and affirm the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit. The decision of the court is unanimous but Justice Kagan took no part in the consideration or decision of this case.
